ai_strategy_lead.name: Raju Gottumukkala
ai_strategy_lead.title: Director, Center for Applied Artificial Intelligence (CAAI); Director, AHeAD Center

documents.title: UL Lafayette Guidance on Generative Artificial Intelligence in the Classroom
Artificial Intelligence Guidelines (Office of Communications & Marketing)

ai_strategy.summary: UL Lafayette has no single published campus-wide AI strategy document but has built a coherent AI posture through its Center for Applied Artificial Intelligence (CAAI), NSF-funded AHeAD Center, AI4ALL workforce program, UL System AI literacy microcredential, and multiple unit-level guidelines (Academic Affairs, Graduate School, Office of Communications & Marketing). President Ramesh Kolluru (appointed February 2026), formerly VP for Research, founded the Informatics Research Institute and is a nationally recognized leader in data science, AI, and innovation, which positions AI as a strategic priority embedded across research, workforce development, and state economic strategy.

key_provisions: Faculty discretion on classroom AI use; default prohibition with permitted uses subject to syllabus policy
Students are responsible for accuracy, bias, and integrity of AI-generated content
Transparency and citation of AI usage required when permitted
CRAAFTED prompt-engineering checklist (Context, Role, Assignment, Audience, Format, Tone, Exemplar, Details) for responsible prompting
FERPA- and HIPAA-protected data may not be entered into AI tools; no proprietary data on students, employees, or patients
AI-generated work may not be presented as human-authored per OCM guidelines
Graduate students must disclose AI use in research with faculty advisor oversight
ai_policy.summary: UL Lafayette takes a cautious, faculty-discretion approach: individual courses default to prohibiting AI-generated submissions as academic-integrity violations, but the university publishes multiple layers of guidance that allow and scaffold permitted uses with mandatory transparency, data-privacy safeguards (FERPA/HIPAA), and ethics framing. There is no unified campus-wide acceptable-use mandate; guidance is distributed across Academic Affairs, the Graduate School, the Office of Communications & Marketing, and Distance Learning.

bodies.name: Information Technology Cabinet
bodies.scope: Approves university-wide IT policies, standards, and budgets

bodies.name: Instructional Technology Advisory Council (ITAC)
bodies.scope: Monthly body covering teaching-technology initiatives, including AI-in-classroom guidance

bodies.name: Data Standards Subcommittee (ERP Governance)
bodies.scope: Data governance for enterprise systems; relevant to AI data use

bodies.name: Center for Applied Artificial Intelligence (CAAI)
bodies.scope: Applied AI research, workforce development, and innovation platform under the Informatics Research Institute

governance_structure.summary: UL Lafayette has no single dedicated campus AI council or task force. AI governance is distributed: the IT Cabinet and ITAC handle enterprise and instructional technology decisions, the ERP Data Standards Subcommittee governs data use, and the Center for Applied Artificial Intelligence (CAAI) operates as the university's de facto AI strategy and research office. Unit-level guideline ownership sits with Academic Affairs, the Graduate School, and the Office of Communications & Marketing.

initiatives.title: Center for Applied Artificial Intelligence (CAAI) launch
initiatives.description: UL Lafayette established the Center for Applied Artificial Intelligence (CAAI) under the Informatics Research Institute as a successor to the NSF-funded Center for Visual and Decision Informatics (CVDI, 2012-2023). CAAI pursues use-driven AI research, AI-ready workforce training, and an innovation platform for the regional economy, led by Dr. Raju Gottumukkala.
initiatives.significance: Positions UL Lafayette as a regional hub for applied AI research and workforce development, building on a decade of NSF industry-university center leadership to compete for the next generation of applied AI funding.

investment.funding_source: University of Louisiana at Lafayette; carryover from prior NSF CVDI center funding
investment.description: CAAI builds on NSF CVDI assets (2012-2023) and is positioned as UL Lafayette's next major applied-research center; specific founding investment not disclosed.

collaborations.description: CAAI convenes AI researchers, domain experts, industry specialists, and community stakeholders to address real-world challenges.

initiatives.policy_implications: Signals institutional commitment to applied AI as a research priority and workforce pipeline.

initiatives.title: AHeAD Center (Accessible Healthcare through AI-Augmented Decisions)
initiatives.description: UL Lafayette won an NSF Industry-University Cooperative Research Center (IUCRC) planning grant and is establishing the AHeAD Center to develop AI-augmented decision-support tools for healthcare, led by CAAI director Raju Gottumukkala, with co-directors at Tulane, Georgia Tech, and University of Florida. Dozens of industry members have signed on.
initiatives.significance: First NSF IUCRC led by UL Lafayette focused on AI in healthcare; places the university in the national network of industry-university AI research centers.

investment.funding_source: NSF IUCRC planning grant; industry membership fees; partner-university contributions
investment.description: NSF planning grant amount not disclosed publicly; AHeAD will be funded by industry membership fees plus future NSF IUCRC Phase I/II awards.

collaborations.description: Multi-university IUCRC with co-directors at each partner site.
Dozens of industry members support the center through the IUCRC model.
NSF IUCRC program backs the center structure.

initiatives.policy_implications: Addresses human-AI interaction, privacy-preserving AI, and robustness of AI models as core research themes.

initiatives.title: Secure AI sandbox at LITE Center with Meta Llama 3
initiatives.description: CAAI is building a secure, locally hosted AI sandbox at the Louisiana Immersive Technologies Enterprise (LITE) Center, enabling students, faculty, and local industry partners to experiment with open-weight LLMs including Meta's Llama 3 without exposing data to third-party cloud services.
initiatives.significance: Rare example of an R1 building a self-hosted open-weight LLM environment for privacy-sensitive experimentation, positioning UL Lafayette's AI sandbox as regional infrastructure for local startups and researchers.

investment.description: Amount not disclosed; runs on existing CAAI compute and LITE Center facilities.

collaborations.description: Local industry partners and spinoffs use the sandbox for privacy-preserving open-weight LLM work.
Spinoffs operate from the Secure AI Sandbox for LLM experimentation.

tools_and_vendors.name: Llama 3
tools_and_vendors.role: Open-weight LLM hosted locally in the sandbox

initiatives.policy_implications: Demonstrates a practical institutional answer to the data-privacy concerns around commercial cloud LLMs.

initiatives.title: UL System Empowering AI Literacy for Students microcredential
initiatives.description: Free, self-paced AI literacy microcredential developed by faculty and staff across the University of Louisiana System (including UL Lafayette), launched Spring 2025, covering AI history, best practices, ethics, responsible use, and data privacy for all enrolled students.
initiatives.significance: System-wide AI literacy baseline for 80,000+ students across nine UL System campuses; UL Lafayette integrates it into courses and faculty development.

investment.description: System-developed, free to all enrolled students; specific development cost not disclosed.

collaborations.description: Co-developed by faculty/staff across the UL System's member institutions.

initiatives.policy_implications: Sets a consistent AI literacy and ethics baseline for all students across a nine-campus system.

initiatives.title: AI4ALL Workforce Development Program
initiatives.description: CAAI-operated workforce program aimed at lowering barriers to entry into AI careers, equipping individuals from all backgrounds with in-demand AI skills regardless of prior experience or educational attainment.
initiatives.significance: Bridges equity/access and workforce pipeline goals, extending AI training beyond degree-seeking students into the broader regional labor market.

collaborations.description: Pipeline initiative coordinated with local industry.

initiatives.policy_implications: Extends AI readiness to non-traditional learners and workforce re-skilling.

initiatives.title: UL Lafayette Data Science & AI Bootcamp (Institute of Data partnership)
initiatives.description: A 24-week part-time professional bootcamp co-delivered with the Institute of Data covering statistics, data analysis, SQL, ML, NLP, deep learning, and cloud computing; graduates receive both a UL Lafayette Certificate of Completion and an Institute of Data industry certification. Advertised 93% job placement rate within 180 days.
initiatives.significance: Non-degree pathway for career changers into AI/data science, expanding UL Lafayette's workforce role beyond traditional degree programs.

investment.description: Revenue-sharing partnership with an external bootcamp provider.

collaborations.description: Continuing-education partnership; Institute of Data provides curriculum and industry certification.

initiatives.policy_implications: Extends access to AI skills training to non-matriculated adult learners.

initiatives.title: Guidance on Generative AI in the Classroom
initiatives.description: Academic Affairs Division guidance that outlines a default prohibition on AI-generated submissions, faculty discretion to permit uses, student responsibility for accuracy/bias, mandatory transparency, and sample syllabus statements ranging from prohibitive to permissive.
initiatives.significance: Establishes a distributed, faculty-discretion model for AI use, with layered guidance rather than a single binding campus-wide policy.

investment.description: Policy-drafting effort; no dedicated budget disclosed.

collaborations.description: Internal Academic Affairs guidance.

initiatives.policy_implications: Default prohibition with permitted-use sample syllabi creates uneven classroom experiences.

initiatives.title: Graduate School Generative AI Guidelines
initiatives.description: Graduate School guidelines emphasizing authorial transparency, faculty advisor oversight of AI use in research, intellectual property protection, academic integrity, and ethical application pairing AI with human expertise.
initiatives.significance: Addresses research integrity distinct from classroom use, with a more comprehensive posture than the undergraduate classroom guidance.

collaborations.description: Internal Graduate School guidance.

initiatives.policy_implications: Embeds advisor oversight and disclosure requirements in graduate research workflows.

initiatives.title: Office of Communications & Marketing AI Guidelines
initiatives.description: OCM guidelines permitting AI for brainstorming, drafts, SEO, social, and productivity tasks while explicitly prohibiting AI-generated visual content, AI fact-checking, and any entry of FERPA/HIPAA/state-federal protected data into AI systems; requires human authorship and disclosure.
initiatives.significance: Unit-level data-privacy-forward AI policy that explicitly addresses state/federal compliance for university communications staff.

collaborations.description: Internal OCM guidance.

initiatives.policy_implications: Operationalizes FERPA/HIPAA compliance for generative-AI use in university communications.

initiatives.title: AI for Louisiana Medicaid fraud detection
initiatives.description: UL Lafayette is developing an AI and data-analysis tool for the Louisiana Department of Health to identify fraud, waste, and abuse in the Medicaid program. The tool is trained on national fraud data and peer-reviewed literature, then applied to Louisiana Medicaid data with staff verification of findings; built under existing state-university computing contract so no additional cost to LDH.
initiatives.significance: High-visibility state-government AI deployment directly tied to a university research contract, showcasing R1 applied-AI work for public-sector program integrity.

investment.funding_source: Louisiana Department of Health (within existing UL Lafayette contract)
investment.description: Cost absorbed inside existing state computing contract, not a new appropriation.

collaborations.description: University builds and state agency deploys and validates findings.

initiatives.policy_implications: Human-in-the-loop verification of AI findings before program action.

initiatives.title: AI and ML concentrations in Computer Science and Informatics
initiatives.description: UL Lafayette's School of Computing & Informatics delivers AI/ML content through core courses (CMPS 420 Artificial Intelligence, CMPS 422 Machine Learning), a Cloud Computing concentration, a Cybersecurity concentration, and graduate options including an MS in Informatics with machine learning tracks.
initiatives.significance: Mainstream integration of AI/ML into existing CS and Informatics programs; UL Lafayette awarded the nation's first master's in computer science in 1959 and has continued to expand AI curricular coverage.

investment.description: Embedded in existing academic program budgets.

collaborations.description: Internal academic program.

initiatives.policy_implications: Maintains AI/ML fluency for computing graduates.

initiatives.title: LONI high-performance computing access (Deep Bayou / QB-3)
initiatives.description: UL Lafayette researchers access the Louisiana Optical Network Initiative (LONI) high-performance computing fabric, including the QB-3 NVIDIA V100-based cluster and Deep Bayou GPU cluster, plus the state's $12.5 million 2022 investment in an AI-capable LONI supercomputer.
initiatives.significance: Provides the GPU-backed compute base UL Lafayette researchers rely on for machine-learning and deep-learning work, without UL Lafayette having to stand up an exclusively campus-local supercomputer.

investment.description: State invested $12.5M in 2022 for a new AI-capable LONI supercomputer accessible to member institutions including UL Lafayette.

collaborations.description: LONI is a shared fabric across Louisiana's R1 and R2 institutions.
State-funded HPC infrastructure.

tools_and_vendors.name: QB-3 / Deep Bayou GPU cluster
tools_and_vendors.role: Shared HPC for ML and deep learning

initiatives.title: NSF CAIG grant: AI for coastal phytoplankton dynamics
initiatives.description: UL Lafayette is a participant in the NSF Collaborations in AI and Geosciences (CAIG) program (over $20M total across 25 projects) with a project titled 'Empowering Artificial Intelligence to Reveal Phytoplankton Community Dynamics in Coastal Oceans'.
initiatives.significance: Applies AI to coastal-ocean geoscience, leveraging UL Lafayette's domain strengths and extending its AI research portfolio into environmental modeling.

investment.description: Individual award amount not disclosed publicly; part of $20M+ CAIG cohort.

collaborations.description: NSF-funded CAIG project.

initiatives.title: Autonomous Recovery from Cyber Attacks (ARC) research project
initiatives.description: $12 million federal appropriation for a joint UL Lafayette and Radiance Technologies research project developing AI-enabled technologies that help systems autonomously detect and recover from cyberattacks.
initiatives.significance: Major federal cybersecurity-AI research investment tying UL Lafayette's cybersecurity and AI capabilities to national-security priorities.

investment.description: $12M federal appropriation for the ARC project.

collaborations.description: Joint research on autonomous cyber recovery.
Federally funded national-security project.

initiatives.title: Compliance with Louisiana Executive Order JML 25-109 (AI use in state agencies)
initiatives.description: UL Lafayette, as a Louisiana public higher-education institution, operates under Governor Jeff Landry's amended Executive Order JML 25-109 banning free AI tools operated by hostile foreign governments (including CCP-linked systems like DeepSeek) and requiring CIO or agency-head approval of state AI procurement.
initiatives.significance: Illustrates how state-level executive action is reshaping campus AI procurement in Louisiana; UL Lafayette must route AI decisions through its CIO and align with state and Board of Regents guidance.

investment.description: No new appropriation; compliance with state executive order.

collaborations.description: State policy cascade through the Board of Regents to system institutions.

initiatives.policy_implications: Prohibits specific foreign-operated AI tools and centralizes AI-procurement approval through the CIO.

computing_infrastructure.supercomputer_name: LONI (shared; includes QB-3 and Deep Bayou GPU clusters)
computing_infrastructure.summary: UL Lafayette's primary AI compute is delivered through the Louisiana Optical Network Initiative (LONI), a shared state-funded HPC fabric across Louisiana R1/R2s, including the NVIDIA V100-based QB-3 cluster and Deep Bayou GPU cluster (NSF CC* award #2020446), with a $12.5M state investment in 2022 targeting expanded AI capability. UL Lafayette supplements LONI with on-campus HPC resources (an on-campus HPC cluster, Posit Cloud, Google Colab, Jupyter services) and a planned secure AI sandbox running open-weight LLMs (Llama 3) at the LITE Center. Researchers also access Science DMZ infrastructure with 80GE backbone connectivity for data-intensive workloads.

regulatory_context.summary: Louisiana has an active AI policy stack affecting higher education: Governor Jeff Landry's Executive Order JML 25-109 (amended October 2025) bans free AI tools from hostile foreign governments (including DeepSeek and other CCP-linked systems) in state agencies and universities, and requires CIO or agency-head approval for state AI procurement. The Louisiana Board of Regents adopted a Policy on Responsible, Ethical, Secure Use of AI on October 22, 2025, and established an ongoing Artificial Intelligence Committee chaired by Regent T. Jay Seale III. The UL System, through its Board and system office, is cascading those standards to member institutions and has launched a system-wide student AI literacy microcredential. The Louisiana Division of Administration also publishes an Artificial Intelligence Acceptable Use Policy for state agencies.

controversies_and_litigation.summary: President E. Joseph Savoie stepped down abruptly on July 30, 2025 after 17 years, with the UL System Board announcing he would retain $510,000/year as president emeritus through December before transitioning to the College of Education as a tenured faculty member after a paid sabbatical. The transition drew public scrutiny in Louisiana press. Not AI-specific, but shaped the leadership context for AI strategy handoff to interim president (then permanent president) Ramesh Kolluru.

narrative_summary:
The University of Louisiana at Lafayette is an R1 public research university (Carnegie R1, ~19,700 enrollment) in the University of Louisiana System whose AI posture is anchored in research rather than enterprise rollouts. The Center for Applied Artificial Intelligence (CAAI), stood up in 2023 as a successor to the decade-long NSF CVDI industry-university center, is the institution's strategic AI hub, directed by Dr. Raju Gottumukkala. CAAI runs applied AI research, the AI4ALL workforce program, a planned secure open-weight-LLM sandbox at the LITE Center (running Meta Llama 3 locally for privacy), and the newly announced NSF IUCRC planning-grant AHeAD Center on AI-augmented healthcare decisions co-led with Tulane, Georgia Tech, and the University of Florida. Major federal funding in 2025 included a $12M ARC project with Radiance Technologies on AI-enabled autonomous cyber recovery, plus NSF CAIG geosciences AI work and a high-profile state contract to build AI fraud-detection tooling for the Louisiana Department of Health's Medicaid program. Research compute runs primarily through the shared LONI HPC fabric (QB-3, Deep Bayou, plus a $12.5M 2022 state AI-supercomputer investment) rather than a dedicated campus supercomputer.

On policy, UL Lafayette has no single campus-wide AI strategy or acceptable-use mandate. Instead, a distributed stack of unit-level guidance covers the Academic Affairs classroom (cautious, faculty-discretion default with sample syllabi), the Graduate School (comprehensive, emphasizing disclosure and advisor oversight for research), the Office of Communications & Marketing (comprehensive, FERPA/HIPAA-aware for administrative use), and Distance Learning's AI Tools & Use hub (with the CRAAFTED prompting checklist). Governance sits across the IT Cabinet, ITAC, the ERP Data Standards Subcommittee, and CAAI, rather than a dedicated AI council. At the system/state level, the UL System's free Empowering AI Literacy microcredential (Spring 2025) is scaling to 80,000+ students, the Louisiana Board of Regents adopted a Responsible, Ethical, Secure AI policy in October 2025 with a standing AI Committee, and Governor Landry's Executive Order JML 25-109 prohibits hostile-foreign AI tools and centralizes procurement approval. Leadership changes in 2025-2026 (President Savoie's abrupt July 2025 departure, interim tenure by Jaimie Hebert, and Ramesh Kolluru's February 2026 appointment as the seventh president) place an AI-fluent research leader atop the institution, with CIO Gene Fields handling IT/procurement compliance. Gaps include the absence of a dedicated chief AI officer, no publicly disclosed campus-wide commercial LLM platform (e.g., Copilot, ChatGPT Edu), and no published unified AI strategy document.
